Scenario.title: Operation Spalax: Targeting Communications Infrastructure
Scenario.overview: Communications Industry Complex A sophisticated multi-stage attack targeting communications infrastructure, leveraging spearphishing, zero-day exploits, and network manipulation to compromise critical systems and disrupt operations.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Logon Session, Script, Application Log, Windows Registry, OS Process, Cloud Instance, Network Traffic, Credential Store, File, Process
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Logon Session Creation, Script Execution, Application Log Content, Windows Registry Key Creation, Process Injection, Process Creation, Network Traffic Flow, Pass the Hash, File Modification, IDE Tunneling, Network Traffic Content

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.description: The attacker manipulates email headers to gather information on the target's communication infrastructure, leveraging OSINT and network mapping techniques to identify vulnerabilities.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.description: Specialized scripts are developed to intercept and manipulate communication protocols, enabling covert data extraction from networked systems.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.kev: CVE-2016-1010 (Integer overflow vulnerability in Adobe Flash Player)
stage.description: A spearphishing campaign targets communications personnel with tailored emails containing malicious attachments, exploiting known vulnerabilities in communication software to gain initial access.

stage.name: Execution
stage.description: The attacker exploits a zero-day vulnerability in VoIP/UC systems, leveraging malformed log entries to execute arbitrary code and establish a foothold in the network.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: A hidden maintenance script is scheduled via registry modifications to ensure persistent access to communication servers, evading standard monitoring mechanisms.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.description: The attacker leverages misconfigured permissions in application logs to escalate privileges, gaining administrative control over critical communication networks.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: Malicious code is injected into legitimate communication processes to mask adversarial activity, avoiding detection by security tools.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC-670: Software Development Tools Maliciously Altered
stage.description: Stolen credentials are extracted from cloud-based communication devices using compromised deployment tools, enabling further infiltration.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.description: Network traffic analysis is conducted to map internal communication infrastructure, identifying pathways for lateral movement and data exfiltration.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.capec: CAPEC-461: Web Services API Signature Forgery
stage.description: Stolen credentials are used to pivot across communication nodes, granting access to additional systems and expanding the attack surface.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC-675: Retrieve Data from Decommissioned Devices
stage.description: Unencrypted communication metadata and content are captured by modifying files on compromised devices, ensuring sensitive data remains accessible post-compromise.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.description: Intercepted data is exfiltrated via stealthy process creation, masking network traffic patterns to avoid detection by traditional monitoring systems.

stage.name: Impact
stage.description: Malformed network packets are sent to disrupt critical communication hardware, causing operational paralysis and denying services to affected organizations.

Scenario.summary: Operation Spalax executed a complex, multi-phase attack on communications infrastructure, combining social engineering, zero-day exploitation, and network manipulation to achieve persistent access, data exfiltration, and denial-of-service effects. The campaign highlights advanced tactics targeting both technical and human factors within the sector.
